revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.
revision.text: #redirect[[en:越知大権現]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''越知大権現'''（おちだいごんげん）は、[[丹生山地|越知山]]の[[山岳信仰]]と[[修験道]]が融合した[[神仏習合]]の神であ...'
revision.text:
'''越知大権現'''（おちだいごんげん）は、[[丹生山地|越知山]]の[[山岳信仰]]と[[修験道]]が融合した[[神仏習合]]の神であり、'''越知山三所権現'''、'''越知山三所大権現'''ともいう。[[十一面観音菩薩]]・[[阿弥陀如来]]・[[聖観音菩薩]]を[[本地仏]]とする。
== 概要 == [[養老]]2年（718年）[[泰澄]]が越知山で修行している時に越知大権現の神験に遭った<ref>[http://www.moon.sphere.ne.jp/ochisan/den_ama.html 越知山観光開発促進協議会 - 泰澄大師の伝説・ゆかりの地/天谷の鉱泉]</ref>のが、修験道場としての越知山の開山と伝承される。越知山は'''白山修験道'''の支流の修験道場として発展し、泰澄が開基したと伝わる[[大谷寺_(越前町)|越智山大谷寺]]<ref>[http://www.echizen-ohtanji.com/rekishi.htm 越智山大谷寺 - 歴史]</ref>の本宮・奥の院・別山に、[[十一面観音菩薩]]・[[阿弥陀如来]]・[[聖観音菩薩]]の[[垂迹]]神である越知山三所大権現が発展した。
越知山大谷寺は[[越前国]][[福井藩|北ノ荘藩（福井藩）]]主であった[[結城秀康]]の祈願所とて中興されたため、[[江戸時代]]に信仰が盛んになった。
== 廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]による[[神仏分離]]・[[廃仏毀釈]]によって、修験道に基づく越知大権現は廃された。越知山三所大権現の[[神宮寺]]であった越知山大谷寺は当時の[[国家神道]]の越知神社と[[天台宗]]の越知山大谷寺に強制的に分離された。併せて、越知山の[[参道]]の[[石仏]]はことごとく破壊された。
== 脚注 == {{reflist|2}}
== 関連項目 == *[[大谷寺_(越前町)|大谷寺]] *[[白山権現]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:おちたいこんけん}} [[Category:修験道]] [[Category:修験道の神]]

{{Buddhism}} '''越知大権現'''（おちだいごんげん）は、[[丹生山地|越知山]]の[[山岳信仰]]と[[修験道]]が融合した[[神仏習合]]の神であり、'''越知山三所権現'''、'''越知山三所大権現'''ともいう。[[十一面観音菩薩]]・[[阿弥陀如来]]・[[聖観音菩薩]]を[[本地仏]]とする。

越知山大谷寺は[[越前国]][[福井藩|北ノ荘藩（福井藩）]]主であった[[結城秀康]]の祈願所とて中興されたため、[[江戸時代]]に信仰が盛んになった。
== 廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]による[[神仏分離]]・[[廃仏毀釈]]によって、修験道に基づく越知大権現は廃された。越知山三所大権現の[[神宮寺]]であった越知山大谷寺は当時の[[国家神道]]の越知神社と[[天台宗]]の越知山大谷寺に強制的に分離された。併せて、越知山の[[参道]]の[[石仏]]はことごとく破壊された。
== 脚注 == {{reflist|2}}
== 関連項目 == *[[大谷寺_(越前町)|大谷寺]] *[[白山権現]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:おちたいこんけん}} [[Category:修験道]] [[Category:修験道の神]]

== 関連項目 == *[[大谷寺_(越前町)|大谷寺]] *[[白山権現]]

{{Buddhism}} '''越知大権現'''（おちだいごんげん）は、[[丹生山地|越知山]]の[[山岳信仰]]と[[修験道]]が融合した[[神仏習合]]の神であり、[[十一面観音|十一面観音菩薩]]・[[阿弥陀如来]]・[[聖観音|聖観音菩薩]]を[[本地垂迹|本地仏]]とする。越知山三所権現、越知山三所大権現ともいう。

越知山大谷寺は[[越前国]][[福井藩|北ノ荘藩（福井藩）]]主であった[[結城秀康]]の祈願所とて中興されたため、[[江戸時代]]に信仰が盛んになった。
== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]による[[神仏分離]]・[[廃仏毀釈]]によって、修験道に基づく越知大権現は廃された。越知山三所大権現の[[神宮寺]]であった越知山大谷寺は当時の[[国家神道]]の[[越知神社]]と[[天台宗]]の越知山[[大谷寺]]に強制的に分離された。併せて、越知山の[[参道]]の[[石仏]]はことごとく破壊された。
== 脚注 == {{reflist|2}}
== 関連項目 == *[[白山権現]]
[[Category:修験道の神|おちたいこんけん]]

== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]による[[神仏分離]]・[[廃仏毀釈]]によって、修験道に基づく越知大権現は廃された。越知山三所大権現の[[神宮寺]]であった越知山大谷寺は[[越知神社]]と[[天台宗]]の越知山[[大谷寺 (福井県越前町)|大谷寺]]に強制的に分離された。併せて、越知山の[[参道]]の[[石仏]]はことごとく破壊された。
== 脚注 == {{reflist|2}}
== 関連項目 == *[[白山権現]]

越知山大谷寺は[[越前国]][[福井藩|北ノ荘藩（福井藩）]]主であった[[結城秀康]]の祈願所とて中興されたため、[[江戸時代]]に信仰が盛んになった。

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:神仏習合]] to [[Category:Shinto-Buddhist syncretism]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''越知大権現'''（おちだいごんげん）は、[[丹生山地|越知山]]（[[福井県]]）の[[山岳信仰]]と[[修験道]]が融合した[[神仏習合]]の神であり、[[十一面観音|十一面観音菩薩]]・[[阿弥陀如来]]・[[聖観音|聖観音菩薩]]を[[本地垂迹|本地仏]]とする。越知山三所権現、越知山三所大権現ともいう。
== 概要 == [[養老]]2年（718年）[[泰澄]]が[[越知山]]で修行している時に越知大権現の神験に遭った<ref>[http://www.moon.sphere.ne.jp/ochisan/den_ama.html 越知山観光開発促進協議会 - 泰澄大師の伝説・ゆかりの地/天谷の鉱泉]</ref>のが、修験道場としての越知山の開山と伝承される。越知山は[[白山修験]]の支流の修験道場として発展し、泰澄が開基したと伝わる[[大谷寺_(福井県越前町)|越智山大谷寺]]<ref>[http://www.echizen-ohtanji.com/rekishi.htm 越智山大谷寺 - 歴史]</ref>の本宮・奥の院・別山に、十一面観音菩薩・阿弥陀如来・聖観音菩薩の垂迹神である越知山三所大権現が発展した。
越知山大谷寺は[[越前国]][[福井藩|北ノ荘藩（福井藩）]]主であった[[結城秀康]]の祈願所とて中興されたため、[[江戸時代]]に信仰が盛んになった。
== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]による[[神仏分離]]・[[廃仏毀釈]]によって、修験道に基づく越知大権現は廃された。越知山三所大権現の[[神宮寺]]であった越知山大谷寺は[[越知神社]]と[[天台宗]]の越知山[[大谷寺 (福井県越前町)|大谷寺]]に強制的に分離された。併せて、越知山の[[参道]]の[[石仏]]はことごとく破壊された。
== 脚注 == {{reflist|2}}
== 関連項目 == *[[白山権現]]
{{Buddhism2}} {{神道 横}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:おちたいこんけん}} [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:Gongen]][[Category:修験道の神]] [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:Shinto-Buddhist syncretism]] [[Category:untranslated pages]][[ja:越知大権現]][[category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]]

'''越知大権現'''（おちだいごんげん）は、]][[丹生山地|越知山]]（]][[福井県]]）の]][[山岳信仰]]と]][[修験道]]が融合した]][[神仏習合]]の神であり、]][[十一面観音|十一面観音菩薩]]・]][[阿弥陀如来]]・]][[聖観音|聖観音菩薩]]を]][[本地垂迹|本地仏]]とする。越知山三所権現、越知山三所大権現ともいう。== 概要 ==]][[養老]]2年（718年）]][[泰澄]]が]][[越知山]]で修行している時に越知大権現の神験に遭った<ref>[http://www.moon.sphere.ne.jp/ochisan/den_ama.html 越知山観光開発促進協議会 - 泰澄大師の伝説・ゆかりの地/天谷の鉱泉]</ref>のが、修験道場としての越知山の開山と伝承される。越知山は]][[白山修験]]の支流の修験道場として発展し、泰澄が開基したと伝わる]][[大谷寺_(福井県越前町)|越智山大谷寺]]<ref>[http://www.echizen-ohtanji.com/rekishi.htm 越智山大谷寺 - 歴史]</ref>の本宮・奥の院・別山に、十一面観音菩薩・阿弥陀如来・聖観音菩薩の垂迹神である越知山三所大権現が発展した。越知山大谷寺は]][[越前国]]]][[福井藩|北ノ荘藩（福井藩）]]主であった]][[結城秀康]]の祈願所とて中興されたため、]][[江戸時代]]に信仰が盛んになった。== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 ==]][[明治維新]]による]][[神仏分離]]・]][[廃仏毀釈]]によって、修験道に基づく越知大権現は廃された。越知山三所大権現の]][[神宮寺]]であった越知山大谷寺は]][[越知神社]]と]][[天台宗]]の越知山]][[大谷寺 (福井県越前町)|大谷寺]]に強制的に分離された。併せて、越知山の]][[参道]]の]][[石仏]]はことごとく破壊された。== 脚注 =={{reflist|2}}== 関連項目 ==*]][[白山権現]]{{Buddhism2}}{{神道 横}}{{DEFAULTSORT:おちたいこんけん}}]][[Category:Gongen]]]][[Category:Gongen]]]][[Category:修験道の神]]]][[Category:Shugendo deities]]]][[Category:Gongen]]]][[Category:神仏習合]]]][[Category:Shinto-Buddhist syncretism]]]][[Category:Untranslated pages]]]][[Category:越知大権現]]]][[ja:越知大権現]]]][[category:kk]]]][[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]]]][[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:越知大権現]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11636594]]
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Ochi Daigongen''' (越知大権現) is a syncretic deity combining [[:en:Mountain worship|mountain worship]] and [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], particularly associated with {{ill|Mount Ochi|ja|越知山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Ochi|qq=|1=Mount Ochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15259982}} in [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], Japan. It is identified with the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji suijaku]] manifestation of [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-faced Kannon]], [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]], and {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音|1=Āryāvalokiteśvara|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Kannon|13=User:Immanuelle/Āryāvalokiteśvara|qq=}}. It is also referred to as "Ochi-san Sansho Gongen" (越知山三所権現) or "Ochi-san Sansho Daigongen" (越知山三所大権現).

== Overview ==
In the second year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (718 CE), the monk [[:en:Taichō|Taichō]] was engaged in ascetic training on Mount Ochi when he received divine inspiration from Ochi Daigongen.<ref>[http://www.moon.sphere.ne.jp/ochisan/den_ama.html Ochi-san Tourism Promotion Association - Legends of Taichō]</ref> This event marked the establishment of Mount Ochi as a Shugendō training ground. As an offshoot of {{ill|Hakusan Shugendō|ja|白山修験|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shugendō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白山信仰|1=Hakusan cult|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579557}}, the faith and practice at Mount Ochi developed around {{ill|Ōtani-ji (Fukui)|ja|大谷寺 (福井県越前町)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtani-ji (Fukui)|qq=|1=Otanji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439052}}, a temple founded by Taichō.<ref>[http://www.echizen-ohtanji.com/rekishi.htm Ōtani-ji - History]</ref> Within this structure, the main hall (hondō), inner sanctum (oku-no-in), and the "Separate Mountain" (Betsusan) housed the three principal deities of Ochi Daigongen: Eleven-faced Kannon, Amida Nyorai, and Shō Kannon.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Ōtani-ji flourished due to its association as the prayer temple for [[:en:Yūki Hideyasu|Yūki Hideyasu]], the lord of the [[:en:Fukui Domain|Fukui Domain]]. This patronage helped to expand the faith and practice surrounding Ochi Daigongen.

== Meiji Period: Shinbutsu Bunri and Haibutsu Kishaku ==
With the advent of the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], government policies of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Buddhism and Shinto) and [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) led to the suppression of Ochi Daigongen, as it was rooted in Shugendō traditions. The shrine-temple complex of Mount Ochi was forcibly divided, resulting in the establishment of {{ill|Ochi Shrine (Fukui)|ja|越知神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Shrine (Fukui)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as an independent Shinto institution and the continued operation of the Buddhist temple, Ōtani-ji. Additionally, many stone Buddhist statues along the pilgrimage routes of Mount Ochi were destroyed as part of this religious restructuring.

== References ==
{{reflist|2}}

== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Gongen|qq=}}

{{Shinto Topics}}

{{translated page|ja|越知大権現|version=75672862|comment=Imported full ja history}}

With the advent of the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], government policies of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Buddhism and Shinto) and [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) led to the suppression of Ochi Daigongen, as it was rooted in Shugendō traditions. '''Ochi Daigongen''' (越知大権現) is a syncretic deity combining [[:en:Mountain worship|mountain worship]] and [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], particularly associated with {{ill|Mount Ochi|ja|越知山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Ochi|1=Mount Ochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15259982}} in [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], Japan. It is identified with the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji suijaku]] manifestation of [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-faced Kannon]], [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]], and {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音|1=Āryāvalokiteśvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Kannon|13=User:Immanuelle/Āryāvalokiteśvara|qq=}}. It is also referred to as "Ochi-san Sansho Gongen" (越知山三所権現) or "Ochi-san Sansho Daigongen" (越知山三所大権現).

== Overview ==
In the second year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (718 CE), the monk [[:en:Taichō|Taichō]] was engaged in ascetic training on Mount Ochi when he received divine inspiration from Ochi Daigongen.<ref>[http://www.moon.sphere.ne.jp/ochisan/den_ama.html Ochi-san Tourism Promotion Association - Legends of Taichō]</ref> This event marked the establishment of Mount Ochi as a Shugendō training ground. As an offshoot of {{ill|Hakusan Shugendō|ja|白山修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白山信仰|1=Hakusan cult|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579557}}, the faith and practice at Mount Ochi developed around {{ill|Ōtani-ji (Fukui)|ja|大谷寺 (福井県越前町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtani-ji (Fukui)|1=Otanji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439052}}, a temple founded by Taichō.<ref>[http://www.echizen-ohtanji.com/rekishi.htm Ōtani-ji - History]</ref> Within this structure, the main hall (hondō), inner sanctum (oku-no-in), and the "Separate Mountain" (Betsusan) housed the three principal deities of Ochi Daigongen: Eleven-faced Kannon, Amida Nyorai, and Shō Kannon.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Ōtani-ji flourished due to its association as the prayer temple for [[:en:Yūki Hideyasu|Yūki Hideyasu]], the lord of the [[:en:Fukui Domain|Fukui Domain]]. This patronage helped to expand the faith and practice surrounding Ochi Daigongen.

== Meiji Period: Shinbutsu Bunri and Haibutsu Kishaku ==
With the advent of the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], government policies of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Buddhism and Shinto) and [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) led to the suppression of Ochi Daigongen, as it was rooted in Shugendō traditions. The shrine-temple complex of Mount Ochi was forcibly divided, resulting in the establishment of {{ill|Ochi Shrine (Fukui)|ja|越知神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Shrine (Fukui)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as an independent Shinto institution and the continued operation of the Buddhist temple, Ōtani-ji. Additionally, many stone Buddhist statues along the pilgrimage routes of Mount Ochi were destroyed as part of this religious restructuring.

The shrine-temple complex of Mount Ochi was forcibly divided, resulting in the establishment of {{ill|Ochi Shrine (Fukui)|ja|越知神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Shrine (Fukui)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as an independent Shinto institution and the continued operation of the Buddhist temple, Ōtani-ji. Additionally, many stone Buddhist statues along the pilgrimage routes of Mount Ochi were destroyed as part of this religious restructuring.

It is identified with the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji suijaku]] manifestation of [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-faced Kannon]], [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]], and {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音|1=Āryāvalokiteśvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Kannon|13=User:Immanuelle/Āryāvalokiteśvara|qq=}}. It is also referred to as "Ochi-san Sansho Gongen" (越知山三所権現) or "Ochi-san Sansho Daigongen" (越知山三所大権現).
== Overview == In the second year of [[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] (718 CE), the monk [[:en:Taichō|Taichō]] was engaged in ascetic training on Mount Ochi when he received divine inspiration from Ochi Daigongen.<ref>[http://www.moon.sphere.ne.jp/ochisan/den_ama.html Ochi-san Tourism Promotion Association - Legends of Taichō]</ref> This event marked the establishment of Mount Ochi as a Shugendō training ground. As an offshoot of {{ill|Hakusan Shugendō|ja|白山修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白山信仰|1=Hakusan cult|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579557}}, the faith and practice at Mount Ochi developed around {{ill|Ōtani-ji (Fukui)|ja|大谷寺 (福井県越前町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtani-ji (Fukui)|1=Otanji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439052}}, a temple founded by Taichō.<ref>[http://www.echizen-ohtanji.com/rekishi.htm Ōtani-ji - History]</ref> Within this structure, the main hall (hondō), inner sanctum (oku-no-in), and the "Separate Mountain" (Betsusan) housed the three principal deities of Ochi Daigongen: Eleven-faced Kannon, Amida Nyorai, and Shō Kannon.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Ōtani-ji flourished due to its association as the prayer temple for [[:en:Yūki Hideyasu|Yūki Hideyasu]], the lord of the [[:en:Fukui Domain|Fukui Domain]]. This patronage helped to expand the faith and practice surrounding Ochi Daigongen.
== Meiji Period: Shinbutsu Bunri and Haibutsu Kishaku == With the advent of the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], government policies of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Buddhism and Shinto) and [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) led to the suppression of Ochi Daigongen, as it was rooted in Shugendō traditions. The shrine-temple complex of Mount Ochi was forcibly divided, resulting in the establishment of {{ill|Ochi Shrine (Fukui)|ja|越知神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Shrine (Fukui)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as an independent Shinto institution and the continued operation of the Buddhist temple, Ōtani-ji. Additionally, many stone Buddhist statues along the pilgrimage routes of Mount Ochi were destroyed as part of this religious restructuring.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:越知大権現]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:越知大権現]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:越知大権現]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11636594]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ochi Daigongen''' (越知大権現) is a syncretic deity combining {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=mountain worship}} and {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō}}, particularly associated with {{ill|Mount Ochi|ja|越知山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Ochi|1=Mount Ochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15259982}} in {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, Japan. It is identified with the {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|WD=Q3080781|lt=Honji suijaku}} manifestation of {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon}}, {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}}, and {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音|1=Āryāvalokiteśvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Kannon|13=User:Immanuelle/Āryāvalokiteśvara|qq=}}. It is also referred to as "Ochi-san Sansho Gongen" (越知山三所権現) or "Ochi-san Sansho Daigongen" (越知山三所大権現).
== Overview == In the second year of {{ill|Yōrō|en|Yōrō|WD=Q1373061|lt=Yōrō}} (718 CE), the monk {{ill|Taichō|en|Taichō|WD=Q2388439|lt=Taichō}} was engaged in ascetic training on Mount Ochi when he received divine inspiration from Ochi Daigongen.<ref>[http://www.moon.sphere.ne.jp/ochisan/den_ama.html Ochi-san Tourism Promotion Association - Legends of Taichō]</ref> This event marked the establishment of Mount Ochi as a Shugendō training ground. As an offshoot of {{ill|Hakusan Shugendō|ja|白山修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白山信仰|1=Hakusan cult|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579557}}, the faith and practice at Mount Ochi developed around {{ill|Ōtani-ji (Fukui)|ja|大谷寺 (福井県越前町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtani-ji (Fukui)|1=Otanji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439052}}, a temple founded by Taichō.<ref>[http://www.echizen-ohtanji.com/rekishi.htm Ōtani-ji - History]</ref> Within this structure, the main hall (hondō), inner sanctum (oku-no-in), and the "Separate Mountain" (Betsusan) housed the three principal deities of Ochi Daigongen: Eleven-faced Kannon, Amida Nyorai, and Shō Kannon.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, Ōtani-ji flourished due to its association as the prayer temple for {{ill|Yūki Hideyasu|en|Yūki Hideyasu|WD=Q1130929|lt=Yūki Hideyasu}}, the lord of the {{ill|Fukui Domain|en|Fukui Domain|WD=Q903537|lt=Fukui Domain}}. This patronage helped to expand the faith and practice surrounding Ochi Daigongen.
== Meiji Period: Shinbutsu Bunri and Haibutsu Kishaku == With the advent of the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, government policies of {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} (separation of Buddhism and Shinto) and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} (abolition of Buddhism) led to the suppression of Ochi Daigongen, as it was rooted in Shugendō traditions. The shrine-temple complex of Mount Ochi was forcibly divided, resulting in the establishment of {{ill|Ochi Shrine (Fukui)|ja|越知神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Shrine (Fukui)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as an independent Shinto institution and the continued operation of the Buddhist temple, Ōtani-ji. Additionally, many stone Buddhist statues along the pilgrimage routes of Mount Ochi were destroyed as part of this religious restructuring.
== References == {{reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Gongen|qq=}}
{{Shinto Topics}}
{{translated page|ja|越知大権現|version=75672862|comment=Imported full ja history}}

It is identified with the {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|WD=Q3080781|lt=Honji suijaku}} manifestation of {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon}}, {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}}, and {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音|1=Āryāvalokiteśvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Kannon|13=User:Immanuelle/Āryāvalokiteśvara|qq=}}. It is also referred to as "Ochi-san Sansho Gongen" (越知山三所権現) or "Ochi-san Sansho Daigongen" (越知山三所大権現).
== Overview == In the second year of {{ill|Yōrō|en|Yōrō|WD=Q1373061|lt=Yōrō}} (718 CE), the monk {{ill|Taichō|en|Taichō|WD=Q2388439|lt=Taichō}} was engaged in ascetic training on Mount Ochi when he received divine inspiration from Ochi Daigongen.<ref>[http://www.moon.sphere.ne.jp/ochisan/den_ama.html Ochi-san Tourism Promotion Association - Legends of Taichō]</ref> This event marked the establishment of Mount Ochi as a Shugendō training ground. As an offshoot of {{ill|Hakusan Shugendō|ja|白山修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白山信仰|1=Hakusan cult|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579557}}, the faith and practice at Mount Ochi developed around {{ill|Ōtani-ji (Fukui)|ja|大谷寺 (福井県越前町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtani-ji (Fukui)|1=Otanji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439052}}, a temple founded by Taichō.<ref>[http://www.echizen-ohtanji.com/rekishi.htm Ōtani-ji - History]</ref> Within this structure, the main hall (hondō), inner sanctum (oku-no-in), and the "Separate Mountain" (Betsusan) housed the three principal deities of Ochi Daigongen: Eleven-faced Kannon, Amida Nyorai, and Shō Kannon.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, Ōtani-ji flourished due to its association as the prayer temple for {{ill|Yūki Hideyasu|en|Yūki Hideyasu|WD=Q1130929|lt=Yūki Hideyasu}}, the lord of the {{ill|Fukui Domain|en|Fukui Domain|WD=Q903537|lt=Fukui Domain}}. This patronage helped to expand the faith and practice surrounding Ochi Daigongen.
== Meiji Period: Shinbutsu Bunri and Haibutsu Kishaku == With the advent of the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, government policies of {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} (separation of Buddhism and Shinto) and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} (abolition of Buddhism) led to the suppression of Ochi Daigongen, as it was rooted in Shugendō traditions. The shrine-temple complex of Mount Ochi was forcibly divided, resulting in the establishment of {{ill|Ochi Shrine (Fukui)|ja|越知神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Shrine (Fukui)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as an independent Shinto institution and the continued operation of the Buddhist temple, Ōtani-ji. Additionally, many stone Buddhist statues along the pilgrimage routes of Mount Ochi were destroyed as part of this religious restructuring.
== References == {{reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Gongen|qq=}}
{{Shinto Topics}}

'''Ochi Daigongen''' (越知大権現) is a syncretic deity combining {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=mountain worship|1=Mountain worship}} and {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}}, particularly associated with {{ill|Mount Ochi|ja|越知山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Ochi|1=Mount Ochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15259982}} in {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture|1=Fukui Prefecture}}, Japan. It is identified with the {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|WD=Q3080781|lt=Honji suijaku|1=Honji suijaku}} manifestation of {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|1=Ekādaśamukha}}, {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}}, and {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音|1=Āryāvalokiteśvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Kannon|13=User:Immanuelle/Āryāvalokiteśvara|qq=}}. It is also referred to as "Ochi-san Sansho Gongen" (越知山三所権現) or "Ochi-san Sansho Daigongen" (越知山三所大権現).
== Overview == In the second year of {{ill|Yōrō|en|Yōrō|WD=Q1373061|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō}} (718 CE), the monk {{ill|Taichō|en|Taichō|WD=Q2388439|lt=Taichō|1=Taichō}} was engaged in ascetic training on Mount Ochi when he received divine inspiration from Ochi Daigongen.<ref>[http://www.moon.sphere.ne.jp/ochisan/den_ama.html Ochi-san Tourism Promotion Association - Legends of Taichō]</ref> This event marked the establishment of Mount Ochi as a Shugendō training ground. As an offshoot of {{ill|Hakusan Shugendō|ja|白山修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白山信仰|1=Hakusan Faith|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579557}}, the faith and practice at Mount Ochi developed around {{ill|Ōtani-ji (Fukui)|ja|大谷寺 (福井県越前町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtani-ji (Fukui)|1=Otanji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439052}}, a temple founded by Taichō.<ref>[http://www.echizen-ohtanji.com/rekishi.htm Ōtani-ji - History]</ref> Within this structure, the main hall (hondō), inner sanctum (oku-no-in), and the "Separate Mountain" (Betsusan) housed the three principal deities of Ochi Daigongen: Eleven-faced Kannon, Amida Nyorai, and Shō Kannon.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Ōtani-ji flourished due to its association as the prayer temple for {{ill|Yūki Hideyasu|en|Yūki Hideyasu|WD=Q1130929|lt=Yūki Hideyasu|1=Yūki Hideyasu}}, the lord of the {{ill|Fukui Domain|en|Fukui Domain|WD=Q903537|lt=Fukui Domain|1=Fukui Domain}}. This patronage helped to expand the faith and practice surrounding Ochi Daigongen.
== Meiji Period: Shinbutsu Bunri and Haibutsu Kishaku == With the advent of the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, government policies of {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} (separation of Buddhism and Shinto) and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}} (abolition of Buddhism) led to the suppression of Ochi Daigongen, as it was rooted in Shugendō traditions. The shrine-temple complex of Mount Ochi was forcibly divided, resulting in the establishment of {{ill|Ochi Shrine (Fukui)|ja|越知神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Shrine (Fukui)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as an independent Shinto institution and the continued operation of the Buddhist temple, Ōtani-ji. Additionally, many stone Buddhist statues along the pilgrimage routes of Mount Ochi were destroyed as part of this religious restructuring.
== References == {{reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Gongen|qq=}}
{{Shinto Topics}}
{{translated page|ja|越知大権現|version=75672862|comment=Imported full ja history}}
